text: The surgeon has entered the right chest cavity, creating a pneumothorax to facilitate access to the esophagus for the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon utilizes double lung ventilation, resulting in the collapse of the lung due to the induced pneumothorax, providing clear access to the esophagus within the right chest cavity.
The surgeon creates a dissection plane between the pericardium and the posterior anterior aspect of the esophagus, carefully separating these structures within the right chest cavity.
In the prone position, the surgeon navigates behind the esophagus, with the pericardium visible anteriorly in the right chest, facilitated by the pneumothorax.
The surgeon observes direct blood vessels arising from the aorta that supply the esophagus, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to coagulate and divide blood vessels supplying the esophagus, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully releases the lung attachment from the inferior pulmonary ligament, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds caudally along the esophagus, maintaining the dissection plane between the pericardium and the posterior aspect of the esophagus.

The surgeon exposes the diaphragm and creates a plane between the right crus and the esophagus. The lower part of the esophagus is completely immobilized. The view shifts cranially, revealing a clear plane between the esophagus and the adjacent vein.
The surgeon exposes the esophagus, revealing the tracheal bifurcation and the membranous part of the right bronchus adjacent to the esophagus.
The surgeon uses a silk suture to ligate the azygos vein, starting with the proximal side to prevent overfilling.

The surgeon completes the ligation of the azygos vein near the superior vena cava, applying clips before dividing the vein. This is the sole structure requiring division during the thoracic portion of esophageal mobilization.

The surgeon dissects the anterior aspect of the esophagus in the subcarinal region, where minimal space is visible between the esophagus and the membranous part of the trachea. Care is taken to avoid excessive diathermy application in this tight area.
The surgeon extends the dissection further cranially along the anterior aspect of the esophagus. The nodal tissues in the subcarinal region are visibly cleared.

The surgeon places the cleared subcarinal nodal tissues into an endobag, showing the completely mobilized esophagus. The patient is repositioned from prone to supine, and five ports are used for the abdominal phase. The gastrocolic omentum is opened, and a plane is created between the duodenum and the transverse mesocolon.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue around the right gastroepiploic pedicle, ensuring preservation of this main vessel supplying the gastric tube during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the attachments between the anterior surface of the pancreas and the posterior surface of the stomach, extending the dissection towards the duodenum.
The surgeon identifies the gastro duodenal artery crossing behind the duodenum and proceeds to divide the short gastric vessels, which are visible as the dissection progresses upward.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the spleen, minimizing traction on the gastric fundus to prevent undue stress on the stomach during dissection.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament, also known as the lesser omentum, with an incision that is extended cranially towards the diaphragm.
The surgeon exposes the left gastric pedicle, with a visible lymph node located along the common hepatic artery that is planned for removal.
The surgeon divides the attachment of the esophagus to the diaphragm, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon exposes the inferior phrenic vessel, highlighting the need for careful dissection in this area.

The surgeon mobilizes the left gastric vein, clearing nodal tissue along the left gastric pedicle. Previously cleared nodal tissues are visible along the upper part of the pancreas and the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon displays the cleared nodal tissue around the common hepatic artery and proceeds to divide the left gastric artery using surgical instruments.
The surgeon further mobilizes the hiatus, creating a retroesophageal window. The fundus of the stomach is visible through this window.
The surgeon demonstrates the complete clearance of lymph nodes along the superior border of the pancreas, extending towards the spleen.
The surgeon clears nodal tissues along the left gastric pedicle, common hepatic artery, and the course of the splenic artery, ensuring complete removal of lymph nodes from these areas.
The surgeon thoroughly clears nodal tissue, revealing the dissection plane between the aorta and the esophagus. The course of the aorta is visible adjacent to the esophagus.
The surgical field shows the mediastinum with visible aortic pulsations, indicating the proximity of the aorta to the esophagus.

The surgeon makes a skin crease incision in the neck and divides the esophagus. A Ryles tube is attached to the distal segment of the esophagus, which is then pulled into the abdomen through a small incision, bringing out the specimen.

The surgeon removes the esophageal specimen and creates a gastric tube using a stapler. The Ryles tube is attached to the gastric tube, and as the Ryles tube is pulled through the neck, the gastric tube is visibly drawn up to the neck. Finally, a pyloroplasty is performed before completing the procedure.
